overview.description: Plotcode is a 14-stage agentic pipeline that automates the software development lifecycle: from feature request submission (via Slack or Telegram) through AI repo analysis, code generation, CI validation, PR creation, QA, and production deployment — with mandatory human approval at 5 defined checkpoints.
overview.goal: The goal is that a product manager with a clearly stated requirement can trigger a deployment without writing a single line of code. The AI handles analysis, implementation, testing, and documentation. Humans stay in control at every decision gate.

flowSummary.task: Feature request submitted via Slack workflow or Telegram bot
flowSummary.notes: Structured form: title, business need, priority, affected service

flowSummary.task: Request ingested — unique ID assigned, ticket created in Supabase
HITL Gate 1 — initial request reviewed and approved
flowSummary.notes: Slack interactive message with Approve / Reject / Request changes buttons

flowSummary.task: AI repo analysis — clone, diff, impact map, risk assessment, implementation plan
HITL Gate 2 — implementation plan reviewed and approved
Feature branch created, code written, tests added or updated
flowSummary.notes: Self-heal loop: up to 3 retries on CI failure before escalating to human

flowSummary.task: CI runs: lint, unit tests, integration tests, security scan, build
Pull request created with summary, test evidence, risks, rollback plan
HITL Gate 3 — PR reviewed and merge approved
flowSummary.notes: RBAC-enforced: only assigned reviewers may approve

flowSummary.task: Change deployed to QA / staging environment
HITL Gate 4 — feature validated against original requirement
HITL Gate 5 — production deployment explicitly approved
Change deployed to production with monitoring active
Closure — ticket updated, changelog written, Slack notification sent

guardrails.text: The AI never merges a PR without human approval
The AI never deploys to production without explicit human approval
The AI never reads, logs, or transmits secrets or credentials
The AI never modifies RBAC configuration or CI/CD pipeline definitions
The AI never skips a defined HITL gate
The self-healing loop halts after max retries and escalates to a human
Every AI action is logged with a prompt hash, response summary, and timestamp

phases.title: Foundation & Infrastructure
phases.intro: Everything else depends on this phase being solid. Do not proceed to Phase 1 until every item here is checked off.

tasks.text: Create monorepo root with /orchestrator /agents /shared /supabase /infra /dashboard
Initialise Git repo, add .gitignore
Create .env.example with all required keys (no real values ever committed)
tasks.notes: Keys: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, SLACK_BOT_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN

tasks.text: Create docker-compose.yml — Supabase local, orchestrator, agents worker
Verify docker-compose up -d boots without errors
Write schema.sql — requests table with all fields
tasks.notes: id, status enum, stage int, title, description, priority, affected_service, requester_id, repo_url, branch_name, pr_url, plan jsonb, ai_logs jsonb[], hitl_approvals jsonb[], error_log text[], created_at, updated_at

tasks.text: Write rbac_roles table — user_id, role, primary key (user_id, role)
tasks.notes: Roles: product_owner, developer, reviewer, qa_owner, prod_owner

tasks.text: Write hitl_gates table — gate name, allowed roles array, request_id FK
Enable Row Level Security on requests and rbac_roles tables
Enable Supabase Realtime on requests table
Create seed.sql — insert test user roles for demo
Run schema.sql and seed.sql against local Supabase, verify with Supabase Studio
Create a demo target repo (plotcode-demo-app) — simple REST API with tests
tasks.notes: Must have: package.json/pyproject, test suite, GitHub Actions CI workflow

tasks.text: Generate GitHub Personal Access Token with repo + workflow scopes
Verify PAT can create branches, open PRs, and trigger workflow runs via API
Add branch protection rule on main — require PR + passing CI before merge
Create Slack app in api.slack.com — enable Socket Mode for local dev
Add Bot Token Scopes: chat:write, commands, workflow.steps:execute, reactions:write
Enable Interactive Components — set Request URL to /slack/interactions endpoint
Build Slack Workflow — form fields: Request Title, Business Need, Priority, Affected Service
tasks.notes: Workflow posts structured payload to /slack/intake webhook

tasks.text: (Alternative) Create Telegram bot via BotFather, store token in .env
Test Slack message receipt end-to-end: send form → verify orchestrator receives payload

phases.title: Orchestrator — State Machine
phases.intro: The orchestrator never does AI work. Its only job is to advance requests through stages and call the right agent at the right time. Build it as a pure state machine.

tasks.text: Initialise Fastify project: npm init, install fastify @supabase/supabase-js @slack/bolt dotenv
TypeScript config: tsconfig.json, ts-node-dev for hot reload
Define Request type in types/request.ts matching Supabase schema exactly
Implement /health GET endpoint — returns {ok: true, ts: Date.now()}
Implement /slack/intake POST webhook — receives Slack form payload, calls intake agent
Implement /slack/interactions POST webhook — receives button clicks, validates RBAC, advances stage
Implement advance(requestId) state machine function with status → handler map
Implement waitForHITL(requestId, gate) — posts Slack approval message, sets status to awaiting_gate_N
Implement RBAC middleware: canApprove(userId, gate) checks rbac_roles table
Implement callAgent(agentName, request) — HTTP call to Python agent worker
Implement agent result handler — on success update status, on failure log error and escalate
Supabase Realtime subscription — listen to request updates, trigger advance() on status change
Define AgentRequest schema: {request_id, payload, context} — shared between Node and Python
Define AgentResult schema: {success, data, error, retry_suggested}
Python agents expose POST /run endpoint — Fastify calls it, waits for result
Implement agent registry in orchestrator — maps agent names to endpoint URLs

phases.title: Python Agents
phases.intro: Each agent is a standalone class with one public method: run(request). Agents never call each other. They only read/write to Supabase and return a result to the orchestrator.

tasks.text: Install: fastapi uvicorn supabase openai PyGithub python-dotenv pydantic langchain
shared/models.py — Pydantic models for AgentRequest and AgentResult
shared/state.py — SupabaseStateStore class: get_request(), update_request(), append_log()
shared/llm_client.py — LLMClient wrapping OpenAI / Claude with retry and rate limit handling
shared/git_client.py — GitClient wrapping PyGitHub: create_branch(), commit_file(), create_pr()
shared/guardrails.py — validate_diff(): checks for forbidden paths and secret patterns
shared/audit.py — log_action(request_id, agent, prompt, response): writes to ai_logs column
main.py — FastAPI app that registers all agent routers on /agents/{name}/run
Parse incoming Slack payload: extract title, description, priority, affected_service, requester_id
Validate required fields — return error if missing
Write new row to Supabase requests table with status=pending, stage=1
Return AgentResult with request_id
Test: send mock Slack payload, verify row appears in Supabase
Clone target repo to temp directory using subprocess / GitPython
Run git log --oneline -20 and git diff HEAD~5 to get recent context
Build analysis prompt: repo context + request description + ask for: impacted files, proposed plan, risks, estimated complexity
Call LLMClient.complete() — log prompt hash via audit.py
Parse LLM response into structured plan: {files_to_change, approach, risks, test_strategy}
Write plan to requests.plan column in Supabase
Post plan summary to Slack thread as formatted message
Test: run against demo repo with a sample request, verify plan quality
Read implementation plan from requests.plan
Create feature branch via git_client: plotcode/{request_id}-{slug}
Build code generation prompt: existing file content + plan + instructions for diff only
Call LLMClient.complete() to generate code changes
Run guardrails.validate_diff() on generated code — block and escalate on violation
Apply code changes to branch via git_client.commit_file()
Trigger GitHub Actions CI via API call, poll for result
Self-heal loop: if CI fails, read error output, re-prompt LLM with error context, retry (max 3)
tasks.notes: After 3 failures: update status=failed, post to Slack with error details, halt

tasks.text: On CI pass: update branch_name in Supabase, advance to PR creation
Test: deliberately introduce a failing test, verify heal loop fires and fixes it
Read branch_name, plan, request details from Supabase
Build PR description from template: Summary, Changes Made, Test Evidence, Risks, Rollback Plan
Create PR via git_client.create_pr() — base: main, head: feature branch
Write pr_url to Supabase requests table
Post PR link to Slack thread
Test: verify PR appears in GitHub with correct description
Trigger GitHub Actions deploy-staging workflow via API after PR merge
Poll deployment status — update Supabase on success/failure
Trigger GitHub Actions deploy-prod workflow after Gate 5 approval
Poll prod deployment status — notify Slack on success
Poll GitHub deployment status every 30 seconds post-deploy
Define metric thresholds: error rate >1%, p99 latency >2s, deployment health check failing
On threshold breach: post alert to Slack, trigger rollback workflow, update Supabase status
Post deployment success summary to Slack after 5 minutes of healthy metrics

phases.title: HITL Gates & RBAC
phases.intro: Human-in-the-loop gates are the core trust mechanism of Plotcode. They must be reliable, impossible to bypass, and clearly audited.

tasks.text: Define GATE_ROLES map in orchestrator: gate1→[product_owner,technical_owner], gate2→[developer,architect], gate3→[reviewer], gate4→[requester,qa_owner], gate5→[prod_owner]
canApprove(userId, gate) function: query rbac_roles, check intersection with GATE_ROLES[gate]
All /slack/interactions handler calls canApprove before processing any approval
On RBAC failure: reply to Slack with permission denied — log attempt
Seed demo roles in Supabase for demo purposes
Gate 1 message: request summary card + Approve / Reject / Request Changes buttons
Gate 2 message: AI implementation plan rendered as Slack blocks + Approve / Reject buttons
Gate 3 message: PR link + diff summary + Approve Merge / Request Changes buttons
Gate 4 message: staging URL + original requirement + Approve / Report Issue buttons
Gate 5 message: changelog preview + deployment manifest + Approve Production Deploy / Hold buttons
All gate messages include: request ID, stage number, who approved previous gates
On rejection at any gate: prompt requester for changes, allow re-submission from that stage
Every HITL approval writes to hitl_approvals jsonb[]: {gate, approver_id, role, decision, ts, note}
Every AI action writes to ai_logs jsonb[]: {agent, prompt_hash, response_summary, ts}
Every guardrail violation writes to error_log text[] with reason and blocked diff hash
Closure message includes full audit summary: all gates, approvers, timestamps, PR link

phases.title: Guardrails & Safety
phases.intro: Guardrails are not README items. They are code that runs on every AI output before it touches the repo. Make them visible — a guardrail trigger in the demo is a feature, not a bug.

tasks.text: guardrails.py validate_diff(): block writes to /.github/workflows/, /infra/, /supabase/
guardrails.py: block code matching secret patterns — API keys, passwords, env vars in code
tasks.notes: Patterns: process.env.*, os.environ.get, hardcoded credential regex

tasks.text: guardrails.py: block modifications to RBAC tables or pipeline config files
guardrails.py: enforce max file change limit per PR — alert if >10 files changed
Self-heal loop hard stop: after MAX_RETRIES=3, set status=failed, post escalation to Slack, halt all AI action
No secrets in logs: scrub known key patterns from all log strings before writing to Supabase
Implement a demo guardrail trigger — create a test request that attempts to modify a forbidden file
tasks.notes: Use this in the live demo

phases.title: Dashboard & Demo Layer
phases.intro: The dashboard is the demo. Judges watch a request flow through stages in real time. Every state change must be visible within 2 seconds of it happening.

tasks.text: npx create-next-app@latest dashboard — TypeScript, Tailwind, App Router
Install @supabase/supabase-js for realtime subscription
Request list page — table of all requests with current status, updated live via Supabase Realtime
Request detail page — 14-stage pipeline visualisation, current stage highlighted
AI plan panel — renders the analysis_agent JSON plan as formatted markdown
Audit trail panel — shows every HITL approval and AI action in chronological order
GitHub PR link card — status, CI badge, diff size, link to GitHub
Guardrail event feed — real-time list of any guardrail triggers with reason and resolution
Status badges: colour-coded per stage type (Agent=purple, HITL=orange, Deploy=green, Failed=red)
Mobile responsive — judges may view on phones
Create demo-ready Slack workspace with 3 bots/users representing different RBAC roles
Seed demo target repo with a simple GET /health + GET /users API
Write the demo script: exact Slack message to type, exact sequence of button clicks
Rehearse full demo flow minimum 5 times — time each run
Prepare a guardrail trigger demo request that shows the system blocking a dangerous operation
Prepare a self-heal demo: deliberately break a test, show AI fixing it
Record a backup video of the full demo in case of live network issues
Happy path test: submit request → approve all 5 gates → verify deployment completes
Rejection test: reject at Gate 2, verify no code is written, verify requester is notified
Self-heal test: inject CI failure, verify 3 retry attempts, verify escalation on 3rd failure
Guardrail test: request that tries to modify .github/workflows — verify block and audit log
RBAC test: attempt Gate 1 approval with a user who only has qa_owner role — verify rejection
Concurrent requests test: submit 2 requests simultaneously — verify they do not interfere
Network timeout test: kill agent mid-run — verify orchestrator detects failure, sets status=failed
Analysis prompt: test against 5 different request types, verify plan quality is consistently useful
Coder prompt: verify generated code passes ESLint and existing test suite on first attempt >70%
PR description prompt: verify output is structured, readable, and contains all required sections
Document all prompts in /agents/prompts/ as .md files — this is a deliverable

deliverables.text: README.md: project overview, architecture diagram, quick start, environment variables
SLACK_WORKFLOW.md: how the Slack workflow is configured, screenshots, setup instructions
PROMPTS.md: every AI prompt used, with rationale for each design decision
RBAC.md: role definitions, who can do what at each gate, how to assign roles
GUARDRAILS.md: list of all guardrails, what they check, what happens on trigger
ARCHITECTURE.md: system diagram, component responsibilities, data flow, state machine definition
DEMO_SCRIPT.md: exact steps to reproduce the demo, with expected outputs at each stage

techStack.reason: Fast webhooks, excellent TypeScript, easy Supabase client
Best AI/ML library support, clean async, Pydantic validation
Best code quality; fallback to Claude Sonnet if rate-limited
Realtime out of the box, free tier, RLS for RBAC, Studio UI
Best-in-class, judges understand it, free for public repos
Official SDK, interactive messages, Socket Mode for local dev
Fast to build, SSR, Supabase Realtime works natively
One command to start everything for demo
One-click deploy, free tier sufficient for demo

winMoments.title: Moment 1 — The self-heal loop fires
winMoments.body: Introduce a deliberate test failure in the demo repo before the presentation. When the coder agent generates the fix and CI fails, show the console live. Watch the agent read the error output, re-prompt the LLM with the error context, push a corrected commit, and CI turns green on the second attempt. Narrate every step. This is the most impressive thing a coding agent can do.

winMoments.title: Moment 2 — A guardrail blocks a dangerous request
winMoments.body: Submit a second demo request mid-presentation: Move the database credentials into a config file. The guardrail fires before a single line of code is touched. The Slack channel receives an escalation message. The Supabase audit log shows the blocked operation with a timestamp. Explain to judges why this matters in a real enterprise context.

winMoments.title: Moment 3 — The audit trail
winMoments.body: At the end of the demo, open the Supabase table. Show every AI action logged with timestamps and prompt hashes. Show the HITL approval record: who approved each gate, in what role, at what time. This is what a CTO asks for before buying any AI tool. Most hackathon projects have none of it. Plotcode has all of it.
